---
title: "Results"
---

## Cost Efficiency by Firm Type

The central question of this paper is whether firms that the government selects for less-competitive settings (Type 1) are actually more cost-efficient than firms that compete only in public auctions (Type 0). The structural model recovers pseudo-costs from observed bids; comparing their distributions reveals whether the government's discretion is well-placed.

### Cost CDFs: Simple vs. Complex Projects

The chart below shows the **cumulative distribution functions (CDFs)** of pseudo-costs for each firm type. A CDF shifted to the **left** indicates that a firm type has systematically **lower costs** — a higher share of its firms can complete the project below any given cost threshold.

```{python}
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import plotly.graph_objects as go

DATA_PATH = "../../code_jmp/Exploratory R/"

# --- Simple projects: data_densities.csv, drenaje == 0 ---
df_simple = pd.read_csv(DATA_PATH + "data_densities.csv")
df_simple = df_simple[df_simple["drenaje"] == 0].dropna(subset=["c_hat", "F50", "typeb"])
df_simple = df_simple.sort_values("c_hat")

simple_t0 = df_simple[df_simple["typeb"] == 0][["c_hat", "F50"]].copy()
simple_t1 = df_simple[df_simple["typeb"] == 1][["c_hat", "F50"]].copy()

# --- Complex projects: data_densities_drenaje.csv, drenaje == 1 ---
df_complex = pd.read_csv(DATA_PATH + "data_densities_drenaje.csv")
df_complex = df_complex[df_complex["drenaje"] == 1].dropna(subset=["c_hat", "F50_d1", "typeb"])
df_complex = df_complex.sort_values("c_hat")

complex_t0 = df_complex[df_complex["typeb"] == 0][["c_hat", "F50_d1"]].copy()
complex_t1 = df_complex[df_complex["typeb"] == 1][["c_hat", "F50_d1"]].copy()

colors = {"t0": "#2E75B6", "t1": "#F2A900"}
line_styles = {"t0": "solid", "t1": "dash"}

fig = go.Figure()

# --- Simple project traces (visible by default) ---
fig.add_trace(go.Scatter(
    x=simple_t0["c_hat"], y=simple_t0["F50"],
    mode="lines", name="Type 0 — Public-auction-only",
    line=dict(color=colors["t0"], width=2.5, dash=line_styles["t0"]),
    visible=True
))
fig.add_trace(go.Scatter(
    x=simple_t1["c_hat"], y=simple_t1["F50"],
    mode="lines", name="Type 1 — Government-selected",
    line=dict(color=colors["t1"], width=2.5, dash=line_styles["t1"]),
    visible=True
))

# --- Complex project traces (hidden by default) ---
fig.add_trace(go.Scatter(
    x=complex_t0["c_hat"], y=complex_t0["F50_d1"],
    mode="lines", name="Type 0 — Public-auction-only",
    line=dict(color=colors["t0"], width=2.5, dash=line_styles["t0"]),
    visible=False, showlegend=False
))
fig.add_trace(go.Scatter(
    x=complex_t1["c_hat"], y=complex_t1["F50_d1"],
    mode="lines", name="Type 1 — Government-selected",
    line=dict(color=colors["t1"], width=2.5, dash=line_styles["t1"]),
    visible=False, showlegend=False
))

fig.update_layout(
    title=dict(
        text="Cost CDFs by Firm Type — Simple Pavement Projects (no sewage)",
        font=dict(size=14, color="#012169")
    ),
    xaxis=dict(
        title="Pseudo-cost (thousands MXN per m³, 2018 prices)",
        tickfont=dict(size=12), gridcolor="#eee"
    ),
    yaxis=dict(
        title="Cumulative probability",
        tickfont=dict(size=12), gridcolor="#eee",
        range=[0, 1]
    ),
    legend=dict(orientation="h", x=0, y=-0.22, font=dict(size=12)),
    plot_bgcolor="white", paper_bgcolor="white",
    height=420,
    margin=dict(l=60, r=20, t=60, b=100),
    updatemenus=[dict(
        type="buttons",
        direction="right",
        x=0.0,
        y=1.12,
        showactive=True,
        buttons=[
            dict(
                label="Simple projects",
                method="update",
                args=[
                    {"visible": [True, True, False, False]},
                    {"title.text": "Cost CDFs by Firm Type — Simple Pavement Projects (no sewage)"}
                ]
            ),
            dict(
                label="Complex projects (sewage)",
                method="update",
                args=[
                    {"visible": [False, False, True, True]},
                    {"title.text": "Cost CDFs by Firm Type — Complex Pavement Projects (with sewage)"}
                ]
            )
        ]
    )]
)

fig.show()
```

::: {.callout-note}
## How to read this chart

Each curve shows the share of firms (vertical axis) that have a pseudo-cost **below** each cost value (horizontal axis). A curve shifted to the **left** means lower costs — firms of that type are cheaper, on average. Toggle between simple and complex projects to see how the cost advantage reverses.
:::

### What the CDFs Show

```{=html}
<div class="cards-grid" style="grid-template-columns: repeat(2,1fr); margin-top:1em;">

  <div class="finding-card negative-card">
    <div class="finding-label">Simple contracts (no sewage)</div>
    <div class="finding-headline">Type 0 has lower costs ✗</div>
    <p class="finding-body">The Type 0 CDF lies to the <strong>left</strong> of Type 1. Government-selected firms (Type 1) are <strong>more expensive</strong> for routine pavement work — the 76% of invitation-only contracts without sewage work.</p>
  </div>

  <div class="finding-card positive-card">
    <div class="finding-label">Complex contracts (sewage work)</div>
    <div class="finding-headline">Type 1 has lower costs ✓</div>
    <p class="finding-body">The Type 1 CDF lies to the <strong>left</strong> of Type 0. Government-selected firms are genuinely <strong>more cost-efficient</strong> for projects with sewage — consistent with economies of scope.</p>
  </div>

</div>
```

---

## Stochastic Frontier Efficiency Index

The structural model recovers pseudo-costs; the SFA model then decomposes these into a **frontier cost** (the minimum achievable) plus excess cost due to inefficiency. The **Battese-Coelli (BC) efficiency index** is $BC = e^{-\hat{\eta}} \in (0,1]$. A value close to 1 means low excess cost; values far from 1 indicate significant inefficiency.

```{python}
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
from scipy.stats import gaussian_kde
import plotly.graph_objects as go

DATA_PATH = "../../code_jmp/Exploratory R/"

df_eff = pd.read_csv(DATA_PATH + "efficiency_index_comparison_drenaje_sfa.csv")
df_eff = df_eff.dropna(subset=["bc_h", "typeb"])

colors = {0: "#2E75B6", 1: "#F2A900"}
labels = {0: "Type 0 — Public-auction-only", 1: "Type 1 — Government-selected"}
line_styles = {0: "solid", 1: "dash"}

fig = go.Figure()

for t in [0, 1]:
    vals = df_eff[df_eff["typeb"] == t]["bc_h"].values
    kde = gaussian_kde(vals, bw_method="scott")
    x = np.linspace(0.05, 1.0, 500)
    y = kde(x)
    y = np.where(x < vals.min() - 0.02, 0, y)
    fig.add_trace(go.Scatter(
        x=x, y=y,
        mode="lines",
        name=labels[t],
        line=dict(color=colors[t], width=2.5, dash=line_styles[t]),
        fill="tozeroy",
        fillcolor=f"rgba({int(colors[t][1:3], 16)},{int(colors[t][3:5], 16)},{int(colors[t][5:7], 16)},0.08)"
    ))

fig.update_layout(
    title=dict(
        text="Efficiency Index Distribution by Firm Type (Half-Normal SFA, Complex Projects)",
        font=dict(size=14, color="#012169")
    ),
    xaxis=dict(
        title="BC Efficiency Index (1 = fully efficient)",
        tickfont=dict(size=12), gridcolor="#eee",
        range=[0.05, 1.0]
    ),
    yaxis=dict(title="Density", tickfont=dict(size=12), gridcolor="#eee"),
    legend=dict(orientation="h", x=0, y=-0.22, font=dict(size=12)),
    plot_bgcolor="white", paper_bgcolor="white",
    height=400,
    margin=dict(l=60, r=20, t=60, b=90)
)

fig.show()
```

::: {.callout-note}
## Interpreting the efficiency index

The BC index is bounded between 0 and 1. A firm with $BC = 1$ is on the cost frontier — it cannot reduce costs further given its inputs and output. A firm with $BC = 0.80$ uses **25% more inputs** than a frontier firm to produce the same output ($1/0.80 = 1.25$). Higher values (further right) indicate **less excess cost**.
:::

### Efficiency Index: Key Numbers

The table below summarizes the efficiency index by firm type (half-normal model, complex projects with sewage):

| Statistic | Type 0 (Public-auction-only) | Type 1 (Government-selected) |
|-----------|------------------------------|------------------------------|
| Mean BC index | Lower | **Higher** |
| Interpretation | More excess cost | Less excess cost |
| Consistent with CDFs? | ✓ Yes | ✓ Yes |

Type 1 firms exhibit higher efficiency indexes on average for **complex projects** — confirming that the government's discretion aligns with genuine cost advantages when the contract involves sewage work.

---

## Geographic Distribution of Efficiency

The map below shows the **average efficiency index** by Mexican state, weighted by contract volume. Darker shades indicate lower efficiency (higher excess costs). States are identified by their INEGI codes.

```{python}
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import plotly.graph_objects as go
import requests

DATA_PATH = "../../code_jmp/Exploratory R/"

df_map = pd.read_csv(DATA_PATH + "efficiency_index_sfa_drenaje.csv")
df_map = df_map.dropna(subset=["id_ent", "bc_t_weighted_ent"])
df_map["id_ent"] = df_map["id_ent"].astype(int).astype(str).str.zfill(2)

# Fetch Mexico states GeoJSON
GEOJSON_URL = "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/angelnmara/geojson/master/mexicoHigh.json"
try:
    geojson_data = requests.get(GEOJSON_URL, timeout=10).json()
    # Check if feature IDs match INEGI codes; add them if needed
    for feature in geojson_data["features"]:
        props = feature.get("properties", {})
        # Try common property names for state ID
        for key in ["CVE_ENT", "cve_ent", "CVEGEO", "id", "ID"]:
            if key in props:
                feature["id"] = str(props[key]).zfill(2)
                break

    fig = go.Figure(go.Choropleth(
        geojson=geojson_data,
        locations=df_map["id_ent"],
        z=df_map["bc_t_weighted_ent"],
        colorscale=[
            [0.0, "#012169"],
            [0.5, "#6B8DC4"],
            [1.0, "#F2A900"]
        ],
        zmin=df_map["bc_t_weighted_ent"].min(),
        zmax=df_map["bc_t_weighted_ent"].max(),
        marker_line_color="white",
        marker_line_width=0.5,
        colorbar=dict(
            title=dict(text="BC Efficiency<br>Index", font=dict(size=12)),
            tickfont=dict(size=11),
            len=0.6
        )
    ))

    fig.update_geos(
        fitbounds="locations",
        visible=False,
        bgcolor="white"
    )

    fig.update_layout(
        title=dict(
            text="Average Efficiency Index by State (Weighted by Contract Volume)",
            font=dict(size=14, color="#012169")
        ),
        geo=dict(
            showframe=False,
            showcoastlines=False,
            projection_type="mercator"
        ),
        height=480,
        margin=dict(l=0, r=0, t=60, b=20),
        paper_bgcolor="white"
    )

    fig.show()

except Exception as e:
    import plotly.graph_objects as go
    fig = go.Figure()
    fig.add_annotation(
        text=f"Map could not be rendered (network unavailable).<br>Data available in efficiency_index_sfa_drenaje.csv.",
        x=0.5, y=0.5, xref="paper", yref="paper",
        showarrow=False,
        font=dict(size=14, color="#555"),
        align="center"
    )
    fig.update_layout(
        height=300,
        paper_bgcolor="white",
        xaxis=dict(visible=False),
        yaxis=dict(visible=False)
    )
    fig.show()
```

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Notes on geographic variation

State-level efficiency averages reflect the municipalities that conducted public pavement auctions during 2011–2018. Not all states have the same number of contracts, so estimates for sparsely represented states are less precise. The map uses contract-volume-weighted averages to give more weight to states with a higher share of observed contracts.
:::

---

## Summary of Main Results

| Finding | Simple contracts | Complex contracts |
|---------|-----------------|-------------------|
| **Type 1 pseudo-costs vs. Type 0** | Higher (worse) | Lower (better) |
| **Efficiency index (BC), Type 1 vs. Type 0** | Lower (worse) | Higher (better) |
| **Policy implication** | Use public auctions | Discretion may be justified |
| **Share of I3P contracts** | ~76% | ~24% |

The results have a clear policy prescription: **for the majority of contracts** — small, homogeneous pavement projects without sewage — the government's selection of firms leads to higher costs. Switching these to public auctions would reduce procurement costs. The exception is complex projects with sewage work, where the selected firms appear to have genuine cost advantages, likely through economies of scope.
